reflectionStructure.label: Scenario Description
reflectionStructure.guidance: Briefly describe the patient, setting, date, and your role.

reflectionStructure.label: Analysis
reflectionStructure.guidance: Explain what you noticed, what guidance or knowledge helped you understand the situation, and what you did. Use the subcategory prompts from the Reflection Guide (Noticing → Interpreting → Responding → Reflecting).

reflectionStructure.label: Evaluation
reflectionStructure.guidance: Reflect on the patient's response, your learning, and what you would do differently next time.

reflectionStructure.label: Future Goal
reflectionStructure.guidance: Identify one small, realistic next step for improvement as a beginning nursing student. Address all 6 Closing Prompts.

slos.title: Utilize ANA Standards of Practice and Code of Ethics
criteria: A. Demonstrates professional behavior.
B. Develops professional relationships.
C. Provides culturally appropriate care.
D. Uses ethical principles in providing nursing care.
E. Follows standards of nursing practice in providing nursing care.
F. Demonstrates awareness of legal issues in healthcare.

slos.title: Develop a structured plan to reflect on personal nursing process
criteria: A. Demonstrates an awareness of own nursing practice.
B. Composes, as a minimum, a weekly written journal of personal nursing care experiences.
C. Reflects on strengths, challenges, and goals for personal nursing practice.
D. Shares care issues and concerns about nursing practice experience with others.

slos.title: Choose reliable sources of information to support nursing care decisions
criteria: A. Circulatory system.
B. Respiratory system.
C. Neurosensory system.
D. Pain and Discomfort.
E. Hygiene.
F. Activity and Rest.
G. Safety.
H. Medication Administration.
I. Social Interaction.
J. Teaching/Learning.
K. Therapeutic Communication.

slos.title: Specify nursing care situations requiring delegation and leadership
criteria: A. Demonstrates leadership skills at the beginning level of nursing practice.
B. Verbalizes appropriate tasks for delegation.
C. Verbalizes appropriate documentation of task delegation.

slos.title: Practice in the role of professional nurse as part of the healthcare team
criteria: A. Demonstrates ability to communicate effectively with all members of the interdisciplinary health care team.
B. Identifies appropriate referral sources for specific client problems.
C. Uses appropriate clinical judgment and critical thinking skills in providing nursing care.

slos.title: Identify factors that influence access and continuity of healthcare
criteria: A. Cultural factors.
B. Economic status.
C. Literacy.

slos.title: Deliver client-centered care
criteria: A. Plans, implements, and evaluates an appropriate plan of care for each client.
B. Communicates effectively.
C. Evaluates the need for client education and provides appropriate teaching.
D. Demonstrates respect, caring, and privacy when providing nursing care.
E. Advocates for client needs.

slos.title: Use therapeutic communication skills with clients and families
criteria: A. Establishes, maintains, and terminates therapeutic, professional relationships and communication.
B. Practices motivational interviewing.
C. Considers psychosocial concepts of client and family ego integrity when communicating.
D. Demonstrates cultural awareness when communicating with clients and families.

slos.title: Develop a plan of care incorporating evidence-based strategies
criteria: Already being done as part of clinical paperwork.

slos.title: Legal and Ethical Issues
criteria: A. Applies the ANA Code of Ethics in clinical setting.
B. Notices ethical dilemmas inherent in clinical situations and describes pertinent facts.
C. Reflects on ethical dilemmas using ethical principles to identify choices and possible consequences.

slos.title: Personal Nursing Practice Reflection
criteria: A. Self-awareness in action: fidelity; identifies own established patterns of behavior and thought.
B. Feedback behavior: seeks external feedback and assistance in reflective process.
C. Personal and professional reflection: interrelates personal and professional behaviors with relevance identified to both self and the profession.

slos.title: Evidence-Based Practice
criteria: A. Preparation: readily identifies and takes responsibility for own learning needs.
B. Information Retrieval: seeks local resources to answer specific questions.
C. Use of Evidence: provides supporting evidence for nursing interventions.

slos.title: Leadership and Delegation
criteria: A. Leadership Development: provides leadership in the modification of client care towards desired outcomes.
B. Competence: explains the purpose and desired outcome of task and time frames.
C. Delegation: delegates, ensuring tasks are within scope and competence, and evaluates the result.

slos.title: Multidisciplinary Team Collaboration
criteria: A. Team Behaviors: readily consults within the health care team.
B. Planning Behaviors: actively contributes to team work; offers help and assists with problem solving.
C. Initiates/gives feedback in a timely and appropriate manner.

slos.title: Referrals and Community Resources
criteria: A. Identifies client resources in the immediate clinical area and in the community agencies.
B. Assists clients to recognize and overcome barriers to optimal health care.

slos.title: Therapeutic Communication
criteria: A. Demonstrates basic understanding of therapeutic communication strategies.
B. Incorporates understanding of client's/family's perspective into a plan of care.
C. Collaborates with the client and family in care planning.
D. Adapts client education to individual client/family needs and attitudes.

slos.title: Oral and Written Communication
criteria: A. Notices cues from clients that indicate barriers to communication.
B. Collaborative communication of plan of care to client, family, and staff.
C. Documents completely in all aspects of the EHR.

slos.title: Clinical Judgment and Plan of Care
criteria: Demonstrated in clinical paperwork, concept mapping, and clinical paper.

slos.title: Apply the ANA Code of Ethics to care of families
criteria: a. Consciously incorporates each provision of the ANA Code of Ethics and Standards of Practice.
b. Identifies when clinical practices and protocols may be at odds with individual patients' rights.
c. Identifies dilemmas in which individual rights are in conflict with the greater good.
d. Articulates dilemmas and identify stakeholders with pertinent facts.
e. Applies ethical principles to identify choices and possible consequences.
f. Engages in reflection about choices, considering ethical frameworks.

slos.title: Reflect on nursing practice in managing care
criteria: a. Interrelates personal and professional behaviors with relevance identified to both self and the profession.
b. Questions personal and professional established patterns of behavior and thought.
c. Practices self-monitoring strategies for uncomplicated professional and personal situations.
d. Acknowledges possible implications for self and practice with occasional assistance.
e. Participates in a personal plan for self-renewal.
f. Consistently prioritizes based on personal and professional values and principles.

slos.title: Seek information to develop plans of nursing care
criteria: a. Seeks information out of interest, beyond the limits of assignments.
b. Recognizes that information continually evolves.
c. Readily identifies and takes responsibility for own learning needs.
d. Uses assistance effectively to frame questions and implement search strategies.
e. Constructs specific search strategy using appropriate terms.
f. Seeks and integrates current knowledge from other disciplines.
g. Evaluates the arguments supporting opinions.
h. Seeks research evidence to refine own nursing practice.

slos.title: Apply basic leadership skills in the care of families
criteria: a. Identifies characteristics of effective leadership.
b. Engages in self-directed professional development.
c. Understands the consequence of making leadership decisions with limited information.
d. Provides positive and constructive feedback on specific aspects of performance.
e. Explains the purpose and desired outcome of the task and time frame.
f. Provides leadership in the modification of client care toward identified outcomes.

slos.title: Practice as a member of a multi-disciplinary team
criteria: a. Readily consults within the health care team.
b. Works well with team members who have varying points of view.
c. Actively contributes to team work; offers help and assists with problem solving.
d. Gives feedback in a timely and appropriate manner.
e. Regularly and realistically self-evaluates own performance.
f. Recognizes need to manage physical health and emotional stressors.
g. Utilize the knowledge of nursing's role and those of other professions.
h. Demonstrate effective teambuilding and collaboration skills.
i. Communicate in a manner that supports a team approach.

slos.title: Recognize benefits and limitations of community support
criteria: a. Recognizes current and needed resources within the immediate clinical area.
b. Has knowledge of community agencies that provide services for clients.
c. Seeks broadening knowledge of practice needs and resources.
d. Identifies areas for improving health care access.
e. Identifies impact of laws, regulations on resource availability.
f. Assists clients to reduce barriers to accessing optimal health care.
g. Identifies practice issues and policies that impact access.
h. Identifies political and policy making processes to improve health care.

slos.title: Deliver family centered care
criteria: a. Integrates and adapts personal style with expected professional relationship style.
b. Intentionally moves out of personal comfort zones to accommodate patient needs.
c. Consistently sets and respects appropriate boundaries.
d. Adapts care to individual client/family needs.
e. Uses understanding of cultural, economic, environmental and social differences.
f. Analyzes the impact of the culture and history of the community.
g. Incorporates understanding of client's/family's perspective into plan of care.
h. Collaborates with client in care planning.
i. Acknowledges and accepts client/family attitudes.

slos.title: Demonstrate therapeutic communication skills
criteria: a. Establishes goals for client-centered therapeutic interactions.
b. Effectively utilizes verbal and nonverbal approaches for therapeutic communication.
c. Encourages client's and family's communication.
d. Caring apparent through tone and nonverbal behavior.
e. Seeks verbal collaboration with other health care team members.
f. Assesses client's learning needs and variables impacting teaching-learning.
g. Uses appropriate teaching strategies and materials.
h. Spontaneously incorporates health care knowledge into routine communication.
i. Creates individualized health teaching plans.
j. Designs and implements health education programs.
k. Knowledgeable of own communication skills and deficits.
l. Recognizes own cultural biases and inexperience.

slos.title: Implement plans of care based on assessments
criteria: a. Regularly anticipates/observes/monitors a variety of subjective and objective data.
b. Recognizes most patterns and deviations in data.
c. Actively seeks subjective information from the patient and family.
d. Usually focuses on the most important data and seeks further relevant information.
e. Interprets the client's data patterns and compares with known patterns.
f. Develops interactions based on relevant patient data; monitors progress.
g. Demonstrates effective and efficient performance of nursing skills.
h. Evaluates personal clinical performance with minimal prompting.

slos.title: Ethics and Legal Concerns
criteria: a. Consistently demonstrates ethical behavior in accord with the ANA Code of Ethics.
b. Consistently applies ethical principles to identify choices and possible consequences.
c. Engages in reflection about choices and the implications for future situations.

slos.title: Plan of Care Adjustments
criteria: a. Consistently examines personal and established professional patterns of behavior and thought.
b. Appropriately seeks and responds to constructive feedback after providing care.
c. Acknowledges implications for personal and professional growth.

slos.title: Evidence-Based Interventions
criteria: a. Takes responsibility for filling own learning needs, selects new and challenging experiences.
b. Consistently evaluates the evidence supporting opinions and overall evidence supporting a practice.
c. Participates in additional learning experiences.
d. Volunteers to assist peers and contributes during clinical to further positive group learning.

slos.title: Management Principles
criteria: a. Identifies characteristics of effective leadership; understands consequences of limited information.
b. Appropriately prioritizes actions, organizes care in timely fashion, assesses clients accurately.
c. Delegates ensuring task is within scope of practice, competence, clear communication, and agreement.

slos.title: Team Collaboration
criteria: a. Actively contributes to teamwork; shares and reports information appropriately.
b. Maintains positive relationships with staff, works well with varying points of view.
c. Asks for assistance and feedback; gives feedback in a timely manner.
d. Recognizes the need to manage physical health barriers and emotional stressors.

slos.title: Health Resources
criteria: a. Recognizes current and needed resources within the immediate clinical area.
b. Makes contacts with community agencies; assists clients to reduce barriers.
c. Identifies impact of laws, regulations on resource availability.

slos.title: Client-Centered Care Advocacy
criteria: a. Integrates personal style with professional relationship style; accommodates patient needs.
b. Uses understanding of cultural, economic, environmental differences to assess uniqueness.
c. Incorporates client/family perspective into plan of care; collaborates in care planning.

slos.title: Sensitivity and Responsiveness
criteria: a. Aware of own communication skills and deficits, recognizes own biases.
b. Identifies how culture, language, and neuro-disability affect communication.
c. Adapts verbal and nonverbal styles to support client's communication.
d. Accurately assesses and provides patient/family education that is evidence-based.

slos.title: Clinical Judgment
criteria: a. Notes and makes sense of patterns in client's data, compares with known patterns.
b. Develops plans for interventions justified in terms of likelihood for success.
c. Explains interventions; calms/reassures clients; directs and involves team members.
d. Interventions tailored for individual patient; monitors and adjusts as indicated.
e. Independently evaluates outcomes and client's response to care; revises plan.

criteria.description: Understands and follows ANA Code of Ethics, Standards of Practice, and Hawaiʻi Nurse Practice Act. Identifies when clinical practices and protocols may be at odds with the patient's rights.
Identifies dilemmas in which individual rights are in conflict with the greater good. Articulates dilemmas and identifies stakeholders with pertinent facts.
Applies ethical principles to identify choices and the possible consequences.
Engages in reflection about choices, considering ethical frameworks and the implications for future situations.
Interrelates personal and professional behaviors with relevance to both self and the profession.
Questions personal and professional established patterns of behavior and thought. Practices self-monitoring strategies.
Acknowledges possible implications for self and practice with occasional assistance. Participates in a personal plan for self-renewal.
Consistently prioritizes based on personal and professional values and principles.
Seeks information out of interest, beyond the limits of assignments; identifies perplexing questions and seeks answers.
Readily identifies and takes responsibility for own learning needs. Recognizes that information continually evolves.
Uses assistance effectively to frame questions and implement effective search strategies.
Seeks and integrates current knowledge from other disciplines. Evaluates the arguments supporting opinions. Seeks research evidence to refine own nursing practice.
Identifies characteristics of effective leadership. Engages in self-directed professional development.
Understands the consequence of making leadership decisions with limited information.
Provides positive and constructive feedback on specific aspects of performance.
Explains the purpose, desired outcome, and time frame of tasks. Provides leadership in modification of client care toward identified outcomes.
Readily consults within the health care team; sees self as a participant in collaborative interactions.
Works well with team members who have varying points of view; accepts and fulfills assignments and commitments.
Actively contributes to teamwork; offers help, shares information, and assists with problem-solving.
Regularly and realistically self-evaluates own performance. Recognizes the need to manage stressors and asks for assistance.
Recognizes current and needed resources within the immediate clinical area.
Has knowledge of community agencies that provide services for clients.
Seeks broadening knowledge of practice needs and resources; identifies referrals to established local community resources.
Identifies areas for improving health care access. Identifies impact of laws, regulations, and guidelines on resource availability.
Integrates and adapts personal style with expected professional relationship style. Moves out of comfort zones to accommodate patient needs.
Uses understanding of cultural, economic, environmental and social differences to assess the uniqueness of individual clients.
Incorporates understanding of client's/family's perspective into plan of care. Collaborates with client in care planning.
Acknowledges and accepts client/family attitudes. Consistently sets and respects appropriate boundaries.
Establishes goals for client-centered therapeutic interactions. Uses verbal and nonverbal approaches for effective therapeutic communication.
Encourages client and family communication. Demonstrates caring through tone and nonverbal behavior.
Assesses client's learning needs, styles, and variables impacting the teaching-learning process. Uses appropriate teaching strategies and materials.
Knowledgeable of own communication skills and deficits. Recognizes own cultural biases and inexperience.
Regularly anticipates, observes, and monitors subjective and objective data. Recognizes patterns and deviations.
Actively seeks subjective information from the patient and family to support planning. Focuses on the most important data.
Interprets client data patterns and compares with known patterns to develop an intervention plan with rationale.
Demonstrates effective nursing skills while providing for clients' safety. Evaluates personal clinical performance with minimal prompting.

reflectionTemplates.focus: Utilize ANA Standards of Practice and Code of Ethics to identify potential legal and ethical issues in the delivery of nursing care.
questions: A. Demonstrates professional behavior — Noticing: What required you to be especially careful about professional behavior? Interpreting: What standard or guidance helped you understand how to act professionally? Responding: What did you do to show professionalism? Reflecting: What did you learn about professional behavior?

B. Develops professional relationships — Noticing: What showed you that communication and teamwork were important? Interpreting: Why did working respectfully matter for patient care? Responding: How did you communicate with your instructor, nurse, CNA, or provider? Reflecting: What did you learn about building professional relationships?

C. Provides culturally appropriate care — Noticing: What cultural, family, language, spiritual, or personal preference did you notice? Interpreting: Why did that preference matter? Responding: What did you do to respect that preference? Reflecting: What did you learn about culturally appropriate care?

D. Uses ethical principles in providing nursing care — Noticing: What ethical concern seemed important — privacy, dignity, choice, truth-telling, or advocacy? Interpreting: What helped you understand why this was an ethical issue? Responding: What did you do to protect the patient's rights or dignity? Reflecting: What did this teach you about using ethical principles?

E. Follows standards of nursing practice — Noticing: What nursing standard, policy, or expected practice applied here? Interpreting: How did you know that standard was important? Responding: How did you follow safe and appropriate nursing practice? Reflecting: What did you learn about following standards of practice?

F. Demonstrates awareness of legal issues — Noticing: What legal issue did you notice — consent, privacy, documentation, scope? Interpreting: What made you realize this issue needed caution? Responding: What did you do to stay within legal and professional boundaries? Reflecting: What legal point do you need to understand better?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety, well-being, or overall care experience? 2. What patient responses confirmed, challenged, or refined your thinking? 3. Were there cues you initially overlooked or misinterpreted? 4. What would you do the same and differently? 5. What does this show about your strengths and next step toward practice readiness?

subcategories.title: Demonstrates professional behavior
subcategories.noticing: What in this situation required you to be especially careful about professional behavior?
subcategories.interpreting: What standard, expectation, or instructor guidance helped you understand how to act professionally?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to show professionalism in your words, actions, follow-through, or boundaries?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about professional behavior from this situation?

subcategories.title: Develops professional relationships
subcategories.noticing: What showed you that communication and teamwork were important in this situation?
subcategories.interpreting: Why did working respectfully with others matter for patient care?
subcategories.responding: How did you communicate with your instructor, nurse, CNA, provider, or other team members?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about building professional relationships in clinical?

subcategories.title: Provides culturally appropriate care
subcategories.noticing: What cultural, family, language, spiritual, or personal preference did you notice?
subcategories.interpreting: Why did that preference matter for this patient's care?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to respect that preference in your care or communication?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about culturally appropriate care from this encounter?

subcategories.title: Uses ethical principles in providing nursing care
subcategories.noticing: What ethical concern seemed important in this situation, such as privacy, dignity, choice, truth-telling, or advocacy?
subcategories.interpreting: What helped you understand why this was an ethical issue?
subcategories.responding: What did you do, or what should have been done, to protect the patient's rights or dignity?
subcategories.reflecting: What did this situation teach you about using ethical principles in care?

subcategories.title: Follows standards of nursing practice in providing nursing care
subcategories.noticing: What nursing standard, policy, or expected practice applied here?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you know that standard was important in this situation?
subcategories.responding: How did you follow safe and appropriate nursing practice?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about following standards of practice?

subcategories.title: Demonstrates awareness of legal issues in healthcare
subcategories.noticing: What possible legal issue did you notice, such as consent, privacy, confidentiality, documentation, or scope of practice?
subcategories.interpreting: What made you realize that this issue needed caution or clarification?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to stay within legal and professional boundaries?
subcategories.reflecting: What legal point do you need to understand better after this experience?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Develop a structured plan to reflect on personal nursing process.
questions: A. Demonstrates an awareness of own nursing practice — Noticing: What part of your own practice stood out? Interpreting: What did that show you about a strength or area needing more practice? Responding: What did you do in the moment to carry out your role safely? Reflecting: What did you learn about yourself as a beginning nurse?

B. Composes weekly written journal — Noticing: What patient care moment stood out most this week? Interpreting: Why was this experience meaningful, difficult, or surprising? Responding: What was your role? Reflecting: Why is this important to include in your reflection?

C. Reflects on strengths, challenges, and goals — Noticing: What went well, and what felt difficult? Interpreting: Why did that strength or challenge show up here? Responding: What did you do to manage the challenge or ask for help? Reflecting: What is one specific goal for next clinical day?

D. Shares care issues and concerns with others — Noticing: What made you realize you needed support or clarification? Interpreting: Why was it important to bring this concern to your instructor? Responding: What did you ask, discuss, or clarify? Reflecting: What did you learn about asking for help?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety? 2. What patient responses confirmed or challenged your thinking? 3. Were there cues you overlooked? 4. What would you do the same and differently? 5. What does this show about your strengths?

subcategories.title: Demonstrates an awareness of own nursing practice
subcategories.noticing: What part of your own practice stood out to you in this situation?
subcategories.interpreting: What did that show you about a strength or area where you need more practice?
subcategories.responding: What did you do in the moment to carry out your role safely?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about yourself as a beginning nurse?

subcategories.title: Composes a weekly written journal of personal nursing care experiences
subcategories.noticing: What patient care moment from this week stood out most to you?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was this experience meaningful, difficult, or surprising?
subcategories.responding: What was your role in the situation?
subcategories.reflecting: Why is this experience important to include in your reflection?

subcategories.title: Reflects on strengths, challenges, and goals for personal nursing practice
subcategories.noticing: What went well for you, and what felt difficult?
subcategories.interpreting: Why do you think that strength or challenge showed up here?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to manage the challenge or ask for help?
subcategories.reflecting: What is one specific goal you want to work on next clinical day?

subcategories.title: Shares care issues and concerns about nursing practice experience with others
subcategories.noticing: What made you realize you needed support, clarification, or feedback?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was it important to bring this concern to your instructor or nurse?
subcategories.responding: What did you ask, discuss, or clarify?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about asking for help appropriately?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Choose reliable sources of information to support nursing care decisions to individuals.
questions: A. Circulatory system — Noticing: What circulatory finding did you notice (BP, pulse, edema, skin color, perfusion)? Interpreting: What did that finding need attention? Responding: What source or instructor guidance helped you? Reflecting: What did you learn about circulatory assessment?

B. Respiratory system — Noticing: What respiratory finding (SpO2, WOB, RR, cough, breath sounds)? Interpreting: What made it concerning? Responding: What guidance helped direct your care? Reflecting: What did you learn about respiratory concerns?

C. Neurosensory — Noticing: What change in LOC, orientation, pupils, sensation? Interpreting: Why did that matter for safety? Responding: What source or guidance helped? Reflecting: What did you learn about neurosensory assessment?

D. Pain and discomfort — Noticing: What signs indicated pain? Interpreting: What did those signs tell you? Responding: What did you do to assess, report, or manage pain? Reflecting: What did the patient's response teach you?
E. Hygiene — Noticing: What hygiene need? Interpreting: Why important for comfort, dignity, or safety? Responding: What care and guidance? Reflecting: What did you learn about hygiene in nursing?

F. Activity and Rest — Noticing: What about mobility, fatigue, or activity tolerance? Interpreting: What made you think the patient needed assistance? Responding: What did you do to support activity, rest, and safety? Reflecting: What did you learn?

G. Safety — Noticing: What safety risk (fall, aspiration, bleeding, confusion)? Interpreting: Why did it need attention? Responding: What did you do to reduce risk? Reflecting: What did you learn about recognizing safety concerns early?

H. Medication Administration — Noticing: What medication info did you need? Interpreting: Why important for safe administration? Responding: What source, policy, MAR, drug guide did you use? Reflecting: What did you learn about safe medication practice?

I. Social Interaction — Noticing: What about mood, coping, support, isolation? Interpreting: Why did it matter? Responding: How did you adjust your approach? Reflecting: What did you learn about social factors?

J. Teaching/Learning — Noticing: What teaching need? Interpreting: Why important at that time? Responding: What teaching did you provide or reinforce? Reflecting: What did you learn about patient teaching?
K. Therapeutic Communication — Noticing: What communication cue? Interpreting: What did the patient need from you? Responding: What technique did you use? Reflecting: What did you learn?
CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect safety? 2. What patient responses confirmed your thinking? 3. Cues you overlooked? 4. Same and differently? 5. Strengths and next step?

subcategories.title: Circulatory system
subcategories.noticing: What circulatory finding did you notice, such as blood pressure, pulse, edema, skin color, or perfusion?
subcategories.interpreting: What did that finding make you think needed attention?
subcategories.responding: What source, policy, or instructor guidance helped you understand what to do next?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about circulatory assessment from this patient?

subcategories.title: Respiratory system
subcategories.noticing: What respiratory finding did you notice, such as oxygen saturation, work of breathing, respiratory rate, cough, or breath sounds?
subcategories.interpreting: What made that finding seem concerning or important?
subcategories.responding: What reliable source or guidance helped direct your care or reporting?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about recognizing respiratory concerns?

subcategories.title: Neurosensory system
subcategories.noticing: What change in LOC, orientation, pupils, sensation, or behavior did you notice?
subcategories.interpreting: Why did that finding matter for patient safety or care?
subcategories.responding: What source or instructor guidance helped you make sense of it?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about neurosensory assessment?

subcategories.title: Pain and discomfort
subcategories.noticing: What signs made you think the patient was having pain or discomfort?
subcategories.interpreting: What did those signs tell you about the patient's need?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to assess, report, or help manage the pain?
subcategories.reflecting: What did the patient's response teach you about pain care?

subcategories.title: Hygiene
subcategories.noticing: What hygiene need did you identify?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was this need important for comfort, dignity, or safety?
subcategories.responding: What care did you provide, and what guidance helped you know what was appropriate?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about the importance of hygiene in nursing care?

subcategories.title: Activity and rest
subcategories.noticing: What did you notice about the patient's mobility, weakness, fatigue, or tolerance for activity?
subcategories.interpreting: What made you think the patient needed assistance, rest, or more observation?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to support activity, rest, and safety?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about balancing activity and rest?

subcategories.title: Safety
subcategories.noticing: What safety risk did you notice first, such as fall risk, aspiration risk, bleeding, confusion, or equipment concerns?
subcategories.interpreting: Why did that risk need attention right away?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to reduce the risk or report it?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about recognizing safety concerns early?

subcategories.title: Medication administration
subcategories.noticing: What medication did you give or observe, and what information did you need before it was given?
subcategories.interpreting: What made that information important for safe medication administration?
subcategories.responding: What source, policy, MAR, drug guide, or instructor guidance did you use?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about safe medication practice from this experience?

subcategories.title: Social interaction
subcategories.noticing: What did you notice about the patient's mood, coping, support system, or isolation?
subcategories.interpreting: Why did that matter for the patient's care or communication?
subcategories.responding: How did you adjust your communication or approach?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about social factors in patient care?

subcategories.title: Teaching/learning
subcategories.noticing: What made you think the patient needed teaching or clarification?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was that teaching important at that time?
subcategories.responding: What teaching did you provide, or what teaching did you observe or reinforce?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about patient teaching from this situation?

subcategories.title: Therapeutic communication
subcategories.noticing: What communication cue did you notice from the patient or family?
subcategories.interpreting: What did that cue make you think the patient needed from you?
subcategories.responding: What therapeutic communication technique did you use?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about therapeutic communication from this interaction?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Specify nursing care situations requiring the need for delegation and leadership.
questions: A. Demonstrates leadership skills at beginning level — Noticing: What required you to think about time, organization, or who needed help first? Interpreting: What helped you understand what needed attention first? Responding: How did you show beginning leadership? Reflecting: What did this teach you about leadership as a student nurse?

B. Verbalizes appropriate tasks for delegation — Noticing: What task could reasonably be delegated? Interpreting: What made that task appropriate to delegate? Responding: How would you explain the task clearly? Reflecting: What did you learn about safe delegation?

C. Verbalizes appropriate documentation of task delegation — Noticing: What part of the delegated task would need follow-up? Interpreting: Why is documentation important when a task is delegated? Responding: What should be documented? Reflecting: What did you learn about how documentation protects patient and nurse?
CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect safety? 2. What patient responses confirmed your thinking? 3. Cues you overlooked? 4. Same and differently? 5. Strengths and next step?

subcategories.title: Demonstrates leadership skills at the beginning level of nursing practice
subcategories.noticing: What in the situation required you to think about time, organization, or who needed help first?
subcategories.interpreting: What helped you understand what needed attention first?
subcategories.responding: How did you show beginning leadership through preparation, communication, follow-through, or asking for help?
subcategories.reflecting: What did this situation teach you about leadership as a student nurse?

subcategories.title: Verbalizes appropriate tasks for delegation
subcategories.noticing: What task in this situation could reasonably be delegated?
subcategories.interpreting: What made that task appropriate to delegate, and what should stay with the nurse?
subcategories.responding: How would you explain the task clearly, including what to do and when to report back?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about safe delegation?

subcategories.title: Verbalizes appropriate documentation of task delegation
subcategories.noticing: What part of the delegated task would need follow-up or accountability?
subcategories.interpreting: Why is documentation important when a task is delegated?
subcategories.responding: What should be documented about the task, communication, and outcome?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about how documentation protects the patient and nurse?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Practice in the role of professional nurse as part of the healthcare team.
questions: A. Interdisciplinary Communication — Noticing: What cues showed that interdisciplinary communication was needed? Interpreting: Why was structured communication (such as SBAR) important? Responding: How did you communicate with the team? Provide an SBAR you communicated or would communicate. Reflecting: What did you learn about effective team communication?

B. Referral Sources — Noticing: What patient need suggested a referral was appropriate? Interpreting: What made that referral important? Responding: What team members or resources were available? Reflecting: What did you learn about identifying referral needs?

C. Clinical Judgment and Critical Thinking — Noticing: What clinical data required you to think critically? Interpreting: How did you make sense of the information? Responding: How did you apply clinical judgment and feedback? Reflecting: What evidence tells you your decisions worked out?
CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect safety? 2. What patient responses confirmed your thinking? 3. Cues you overlooked? 4. Same and differently? 5. Strengths and next step?

subcategories.title: Interdisciplinary Communication
subcategories.noticing: What cues from team members, the patient, or family showed that interdisciplinary communication was needed?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was structured communication (such as SBAR) important in this situation?
subcategories.responding: How did you communicate with members of the interdisciplinary health care team? Provide an SBAR you communicated or would communicate.
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about effective team communication?

subcategories.title: Referral Sources
subcategories.noticing: What patient need suggested a referral or additional team resource was appropriate?
subcategories.interpreting: What made that referral important for the patient's care or safety?
subcategories.responding: What team members or resources were available, and what patient preferences did you consider?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about identifying referral needs?

subcategories.title: Clinical Judgment and Critical Thinking
subcategories.noticing: What clinical data or patient response required you to think critically?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you make sense of the information and decide what mattered most?
subcategories.responding: How did you apply clinical judgment and feedback from team members in your care?
subcategories.reflecting: What evidence tells you your decisions worked out for the patient?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Identify factors that influence access and continuity of healthcare.
questions: A. Cultural factors — Noticing: What cultural belief, practice, family role, or communication need did you notice? Interpreting: Why did that factor matter for care or decision-making? Responding: How did you adjust your care, communication, or teaching? Reflecting: What did you learn about culture and continuity of care?

B. Economic status — Noticing: What clue suggested that money, insurance, transportation, housing, or medication cost might affect care? Interpreting: Why could that become a barrier? Responding: What support, referral, or discharge planning need did you recognize? Reflecting: What did you learn about finances and health care access?

C. Literacy — Noticing: What sign suggested the patient may have difficulty understanding health information? Interpreting: Why did that matter for safety, teaching, or follow-up? Responding: How did you adjust your teaching or communication? Reflecting: What did you learn about health literacy?
CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect safety? 2. What patient responses confirmed your thinking? 3. Cues you overlooked? 4. Same and differently? 5. Strengths and next step?

subcategories.responding: How did you adjust your care, communication, or teaching?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about culture and continuity of care?

subcategories.noticing: What clue suggested that money, insurance, transportation, housing, work, or medication cost might affect care?
subcategories.interpreting: Why could that become a barrier to following the plan of care?
subcategories.responding: What support, referral, or discharge planning need did you recognize?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about the effect of finances on health care access?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Deliver client-centered care.
questions: A. Plans, implements, and evaluates plan of care — Noticing: What patient need seemed most important? Interpreting: What helped you understand what the patient needed first? Responding: What care did you provide based on patient's condition and guidance? Reflecting: How did the patient respond?

B. Communicates effectively — Noticing: What communication need? Interpreting: Why was clear communication important? Responding: How did you communicate respectfully and understandably? Reflecting: What did you learn about effective communication?

C. Evaluates need for client education and provides teaching — Noticing: What made you realize the patient needed teaching? Interpreting: What did the patient most need to understand? Responding: What teaching did you provide or reinforce? Reflecting: How did you know whether the patient understood?

D. Demonstrates respect, caring, and privacy — Noticing: What reminded you that privacy, dignity, or comfort were important? Interpreting: Why did this patient need particular attention to respect? Responding: What did you do to protect privacy, dignity, and comfort? Reflecting: What did this teach you about respectful nursing care?

E. Advocates for client needs — Noticing: What patient need or concern made you think someone should speak up? Interpreting: Why did that need matter enough to report? Responding: What did you say or do to advocate? Reflecting: What did you learn about advocacy as a beginning nurse?
CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect safety? 2. What patient responses confirmed your thinking? 3. Cues you overlooked? 4. Same and differently? 5. Strengths and next step?

subcategories.interpreting: What helped you understand what the patient needed first?
subcategories.responding: What care did you provide based on the patient's condition and your instructor's or nurse's guidance?
subcategories.reflecting: How did the patient respond, and what did you learn from that?

subcategories.reflecting: How did you know whether the patient understood, and what did you learn?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Use therapeutic communication skills in the development of relationships with clients and families.
questions: A. Establishes, maintains, and terminates therapeutic relationships — Noticing: What did you notice about how the patient or family responded to you? Interpreting: What did that tell you about how to build trust and keep boundaries? Responding: How did you begin, continue, and close the interaction professionally? Reflecting: What did you learn about building therapeutic relationships?

B. Practices motivational interviewing — Noticing: What sign suggested the patient was unsure, hesitant, or not ready? Interpreting: What did that tell you about readiness to talk or change? Responding: What open-ended question, reflection, affirmation, or summary did you use? Reflecting: What did you learn about helping a patient talk through concerns?

C. Considers psychosocial concepts of client and family ego integrity — Noticing: What emotional, developmental, or psychosocial need did you notice? Interpreting: Why did that need matter in communication? Responding: How did you adjust your tone, words, or presence? Reflecting: What did you learn about psychosocially sensitive communication?

D. Demonstrates cultural awareness when communicating — Noticing: What cultural, language, spiritual, or family-related factor affected communication? Interpreting: Why did that factor matter for understanding and trust? Responding: What did you do to communicate respectfully and avoid assumptions? Reflecting: What did you learn about cultural awareness in communication?
CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect safety? 2. What patient responses confirmed your thinking? 3. Cues you overlooked? 4. Same and differently? 5. Strengths and next step?

subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about building therapeutic relationships?

subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about helping a patient talk through concerns?

subcategories.responding: How did you adjust your tone, words, or presence?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Identify potential legal and ethical issues in delivery of health care.
questions: A. Applies the ANA Code of Ethics — Noticing: What made you think about ethics, standards, or the Hawaii Nurse Practice Act? Interpreting: What helped you understand which ethical or legal standard applied? Responding: What did you do to act consistently with the ANA Code of Ethics? Reflecting: What did this teach you about applying ethical standards in real patient care?

B. Notices ethical dilemmas and describes pertinent facts — Noticing: What ethical dilemma or conflict caught your attention? Interpreting: Who were the main stakeholders, and what facts seemed most important? Responding: How did you describe or communicate the dilemma accurately? Reflecting: What did this teach you about recognizing ethical dilemmas early?

C. Reflects on ethical dilemmas using ethical principles — Noticing: What choice or decision point needed ethical reflection? Interpreting: What ethical principles helped you think through possible choices and consequences? Responding: What option did you support, and why? Reflecting: What would you do the same or differently?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. What did you feel most confident about? 2. What were you most concerned about related to the patient's care? 3. How did the patient respond? 4. If you could do this clinical day over, what would you do differently? 5. What did you learn that can apply to future patients? 6. What do you still need to learn?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Reflect on personal nursing practice within the legal and ethical framework of nursing.
questions: A. Self-awareness in action — Noticing: What did you notice about your own behavior, thinking, or habits? Interpreting: How did those patterns affect the way you responded? Responding: What did you do to remain safe, honest, and accountable? Reflecting: What did this teach you about your developing nursing practice?

B. Feedback behavior — Noticing: What made you realize you needed feedback or help? Interpreting: Why was outside feedback important? Responding: What feedback did you seek, and how did you use it? Reflecting: What did this teach you about asking for assistance?

C. Personal and professional reflection — Noticing: What felt personally and professionally important? Interpreting: How did this connect your personal values with the professional role? Responding: How did you manage yourself in a way that supported professional care? Reflecting: What did you learn about who you are personally and how you practice professionally?
CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. Most confident about? 2. Most concerned about? 3. Patient response? 4. Do differently? 5. Apply to future patients? 6. Still need to learn?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Utilize reliable sources of information to support nursing care decisions.
questions: A. Preparation: takes responsibility for learning needs — Noticing: What question or knowledge gap came up? Interpreting: Why did you decide this needed understanding? Responding: What did you do to take responsibility? Reflecting: What did this teach you about preparation?

B. Information retrieval: seeks local resources — Noticing: What specific clinical question needed answering? Interpreting: Which resource seemed most useful (Lexicomp, textbook, staff nurse, chart)? Responding: What source did you use? Reflecting: What did you learn about finding reliable answers?

C. Use of evidence: provides supporting evidence — Noticing: What intervention needed support or rationale? Interpreting: What information helped you understand why that intervention was appropriate? Responding: How did that evidence influence what you did? Reflecting: What did this teach you about understanding why you are doing an intervention?
CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. Most confident about? 2. Most concerned about? 3. Patient response? 4. Do differently? 5. Apply to future patients? 6. Still need to learn?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Develop own leadership abilities and acknowledge delegation as needed.

Interpreting: How did you decide what patient need required attention first? Responding: What did you do that showed beginning leadership? Reflecting: What did this teach you about your developing leadership?

B. Competence: explains purpose and outcome — Noticing: What task needed clearer purpose, timing, or coordination? Interpreting: Why was it important to explain the purpose and expected outcome? Responding: How did you explain what needed to be done, why, and when? Reflecting: What did this teach you about communicating tasks clearly?

C. Delegation: within scope and competence — Noticing: What task could appropriately be delegated? Interpreting: How did you determine whether it was within the other person's scope? Responding: How would you delegate clearly and evaluate completion? Reflecting: What did this teach you about safe delegation and accountability?
CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. Most confident about? 2. Most concerned about? 3. Patient response? 4. Do differently? 5. Apply to future patients? 6. Still need to learn?
