questions: Noticing: What assessment data, patient concerns, or illness-experience cues stood out first? What were you paying close attention to?

Interpreting: How did you begin to make sense of the patient's assessment findings together with the patient's perspective? What knowledge or consultation helped you understand what was most important?
Responding: Based on what you noticed and understood, what plan of care did you develop or help carry out? What actions did you take, and what outcomes were you hoping for?
Reflecting: How did the patient respond to the plan of care? What went as expected, what did not, and what would you do differently next time?
CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. Most confident about? 2. Most concerned about? 3. Patient response? 4. Do differently? 5. Apply to future patients? 6. Still need to learn?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Apply the ANA Code of Ethics to care of families.
questions: Noticing: What ethical or legal concerns did you begin to organize and make sense of? What struck you about this patient's rights or the family's situation? What were you worried about?

Interpreting: How did you connect your knowledge of the ANA Code of Ethics with the specific patient and family situation? Whose values did you consider important — and how did they influence your approach?
Responding: What specific actions did you take in response to the ethical situation? If the ethics called for differed from your own, how did you reconcile that? What evidence supports your decision?
Reflecting: If you had to do it over again, would you do anything differently? Were there cues you overlooked about the ethical dimensions? What guidelines would you give other nurses?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety, well-being, or overall care experience? 2. What patient responses confirmed, challenged, or refined your thinking? 3. Were there cues you initially overlooked or misinterpreted? 4. What would you do the same and what differently? 5. What does this experience show you about your strengths and your next step toward practice readiness?

subcategories.title: Demonstrates professional behavior
subcategories.noticing: What professional expectation or standard was especially important in this situation?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you connect your behavior to nursing standards, accountability, or patient trust?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to act professionally in your words, tone, appearance, follow-through, or decision-making?
subcategories.reflecting: What did this situation teach you about how your behavior affects patient care and the image of the profession?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you able to describe this patient's problem specifically, or was your understanding still general? Were you organizing your day around patient needs and taking pride in that organization? Did you find yourself checking repeatedly to make sure you did everything correctly?

subcategories.title: Develops professional relationships
subcategories.noticing: What cues from the patient, family, nurse, or instructor showed that trust and teamwork mattered in this situation?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was the relationship itself important to the patient outcome?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to build or maintain a respectful professional relationship?
subcategories.reflecting: What helped the relationship go well, and what would you improve next time?
subcategories.selfCheck: Are you beginning to notice that how you interact with team members affects patient care? Were you engaged with the team or still focused mainly on completing your own tasks?

subcategories.title: Provides culturally appropriate care
subcategories.noticing: What cultural, spiritual, family, language, or personal factors did you begin to recognize?
subcategories.interpreting: How did those factors influence what the patient needed or valued most?
subcategories.responding: How did you adapt your care or communication to be more culturally respectful and appropriate?
subcategories.reflecting: What assumptions or biases did you become aware of, and what will you do differently next time?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to see this patient as a whole person with unique values, or were cultural factors still abstract concepts to you? Did you notice the patient responding positively or negatively to how you communicated?

subcategories.title: Uses ethical principles in providing nursing care
subcategories.noticing: What ethical concern grabbed your attention in this situation?
subcategories.interpreting: Whose values mattered here, and how did those values influence your thinking?
subcategories.responding: What action did you take, or what action should have been taken, to protect the patient's rights, dignity, or well-being?
subcategories.reflecting: Were there any ethical cues you overlooked at first? What did this teach you about handling similar situations in the future?
subcategories.selfCheck: Did you need deliberate, conscious effort to recognize the ethical dimension, or did it stand out immediately? Were you beginning to connect ethical principles to this specific patient's situation, or was your thinking still general?

subcategories.title: Follows standards of nursing practice
subcategories.noticing: What standard, policy, or expected nursing practice applied in this case?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was that standard especially important for this patient at this time?
subcategories.responding: How did your care align with safe nursing practice?
subcategories.reflecting: Looking back, did your actions fully match the standard? Why or why not?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you following the standard because you understood why it mattered for this patient, or primarily because it was the rule? Are you beginning to organize your care around standards deliberately rather than just checking items off?

subcategories.title: Demonstrates awareness of legal issues
subcategories.noticing: What legal issue or risk did you recognize, such as consent, privacy, confidentiality, scope, safety, or documentation?
subcategories.interpreting: Why did this issue matter in the patient's situation?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to stay within legal and professional boundaries?
subcategories.reflecting: What legal point do you need to understand better after this experience?
subcategories.selfCheck: Did you feel anxious about whether you were handling this correctly? Were there knowledge gaps about legal issues that concerned you? That awareness itself is a sign of growth.

reflectionTemplates.focus: Reflect on nursing practice in managing care for groups of patients.
questions: Noticing: What changes in patient comfort, condition, or responses did you begin to organize into meaningful observations? What cues signaled the need to modify care? Were you surprised by anything?
Interpreting: How did you connect your classroom knowledge with what you observed? What hunches did you have? How did you prioritize — what was most salient and why?
Responding: How did you plan and implement care modifications deliberately? What outcomes were you hoping to attain? How did you seek and respond to feedback from your instructor?
Reflecting: How is your patient responding? How did your priorities change? What did the patient's responses make you realize about your thinking? What went well and what could have gone better?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety, well-being, or overall care experience? 2. What patient responses confirmed, challenged, or refined your thinking? 3. Were there cues you initially overlooked or misinterpreted? 4. What would you do the same and what differently? 5. What does this experience show you about your strengths and your next step toward practice readiness?

subcategories.title: Demonstrates awareness of own nursing practice
subcategories.noticing: What part of your own practice stood out to you during this clinical experience?
subcategories.interpreting: What did that reveal about your strengths, habits, or areas needing growth?
subcategories.responding: How did you adjust your thinking or actions during the shift?
subcategories.reflecting: What does this situation teach you about the kind of nurse you are becoming?
subcategories.selfCheck: Are you beginning to evaluate your own performance rather than relying entirely on your instructor to tell you how you did? Can you identify what went well without being prompted?

subcategories.title: Weekly written journal of personal nursing care experiences
subcategories.noticing: What patient experience or care moment was most meaningful this week?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was that situation important to your learning?
subcategories.responding: How did you participate in the situation, and what was your role?
subcategories.reflecting: Why is this experience worth writing about and learning from?
subcategories.selfCheck: Are you writing about what the patient taught you, or still mostly describing the tasks you completed? Are you beginning to see clinical experiences as learning opportunities rather than just assignments?

subcategories.title: Reflects on strengths, challenges, and goals
subcategories.noticing: What went well, and what was difficult for you in this situation?
subcategories.interpreting: Why do you think those strengths and challenges showed up here?
subcategories.responding: What did you do in the moment to manage the challenge?
subcategories.reflecting: What is one specific SMART goal you will set for your next clinical day?
subcategories.selfCheck: Did you worry about missing something important during this experience? That hypervigilance is normal at this stage — it shows you care about getting it right. Are you beginning to turn that worry into deliberate planning?

subcategories.title: Shares care issues and concerns with others
subcategories.noticing: What concern or uncertainty made you realize you needed guidance?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was it important to ask for help, clarification, or feedback?
subcategories.responding: Who did you talk to, and how did their input affect your care?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about seeking support appropriately in clinical practice?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you able to describe the situation clearly enough that others understood what you needed? At this stage, your descriptions may not yet be specific enough to get the exact response you need — that skill develops with practice.

reflectionTemplates.focus: Seek evidence-based information for family-centered care plans.
questions: Noticing: What clinical observations or patient cues prompted you to seek evidence-based information? What clinical knowledge was particularly important? What type of knowledge did you need more depth on?
Interpreting: How did you select and organize knowledge from clinical sources? Talk about the pathophysiologic mechanism at work. What findings did you notice that were best understood together?
Responding: How did you organize your care according to evidence-based plans and goals? What evidence supports your decisions? What interventions do you anticipate to prevent complications?
Reflecting: What were the outcomes, and how did they compare to what you expected? What patient responses make you think the care was effective or not? What questions still need investigation?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety, well-being, or overall care experience? 2. What patient responses confirmed, challenged, or refined your thinking? 3. Were there cues you initially overlooked or misinterpreted? 4. What would you do the same and what differently? 5. What does this experience show you about your strengths and your next step toward practice readiness?

subcategories.title: Circulatory system
subcategories.noticing: What circulatory findings caught your attention?
subcategories.interpreting: What did those findings make you think might be happening?
subcategories.responding: What reliable source helped guide your care or understanding?
subcategories.reflecting: What did this teach you about assessing and prioritizing circulatory concerns?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to organize these circulatory findings into a pattern (e.g., BP + HR + perfusion together), or were they still separate data points? Did connecting your pathophysiology knowledge to what you saw require conscious, deliberate effort?

subcategories.title: Respiratory system
subcategories.noticing: What respiratory cues or changes did you organize into a meaningful pattern?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you connect those findings to pathophysiology or likely complications?
subcategories.responding: What evidence-based action or observation did you take?
subcategories.reflecting: What would you watch more closely next time with a similar patient?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to see the respiratory picture as a whole (rate + depth + sounds + O2 sat + work of breathing), or still assessing each piece separately? Could you anticipate common complications, or did they surprise you?

subcategories.title: Neurosensory system
subcategories.noticing: What neurologic or sensory changes were most important?
subcategories.interpreting: What did those cues mean in this patient's situation?
subcategories.responding: What assessment, reporting, or intervention did you do based on those findings?
subcategories.reflecting: Were there subtle cues you might catch earlier next time?
subcategories.selfCheck: Was this a familiar neuro presentation where you could anticipate what to expect, or was it unfamiliar and uncertain? Were you beginning to see the big picture, or still focused on individual findings?

subcategories.title: Pain and discomfort
subcategories.noticing: What told you that the patient was in pain or discomfort?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you decide what kind of pain or discomfort the patient was experiencing?
subcategories.responding: What intervention or comfort measure did you provide, and what evidence supported it?
subcategories.reflecting: How did the patient respond, and what did that teach you?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to see the patient's pain and suffering as meaningful — not just a number on a scale? Did you notice the patient responding positively to your comfort interventions?

subcategories.title: Hygiene
subcategories.noticing: What hygiene or basic care need did you identify?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was this need important beyond just task completion?
subcategories.responding: How did you provide care in a way that supported dignity, comfort, and safety?
subcategories.reflecting: What did this experience teach you about the nursing meaning of hygiene care?
subcategories.selfCheck: Are you beginning to see hygiene care as an opportunity to know the patient and assess their condition, or does it still feel primarily like a task to complete?

subcategories.title: Activity and Rest
subcategories.noticing: What cues showed the patient's tolerance or intolerance for activity?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you connect those cues to the patient's condition and energy needs?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to balance activity, rest, and safety?
subcategories.reflecting: What would you assess more carefully next time before helping a patient mobilize?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to anticipate what this patient would need before mobilizing, or did issues arise that you didn't expect? Could you see the connection between activity tolerance and the patient's overall condition?

subcategories.title: Safety
subcategories.noticing: What safety risk stood out most clearly?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was that risk the most urgent or significant?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to reduce harm or prevent complications?
subcategories.reflecting: What additional safety cue might you have missed at first?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to anticipate safety risks before they happened, or did you respond after the risk became obvious? Did you worry about missing something — and did that worry help you be more thorough?

subcategories.title: Medication Administration
subcategories.noticing: What information did you need before administering or evaluating the medication?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you connect the medication to the patient's diagnosis, assessment findings, and possible risks?
subcategories.responding: What source did you use to confirm safe medication practice, and how did that influence your action?
subcategories.reflecting: What did this experience teach you about medication judgment, not just medication task completion?
subcategories.selfCheck: With conscious effort, were you able to organize your medication knowledge (indication, dose, side effects, nursing implications) into a plan, or did you rely heavily on references? Are you moving beyond "giving the med" toward understanding why this med for this patient?

subcategories.title: Social Interaction
subcategories.noticing: What did you notice about the patient's mood, coping, support system, or isolation?
subcategories.interpreting: Why did these social cues matter to the patient's care?
subcategories.responding: How did you adjust your communication or care based on what you noticed?
subcategories.reflecting: What did this teach you about the relationship between social needs and health outcomes?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to see the patient as a person with a life beyond the hospital bed? Did you know what mattered most to this patient and family, or were you still focused primarily on clinical tasks?

subcategories.title: Teaching/Learning
subcategories.noticing: What told you the patient needed teaching or clarification?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you decide what education was most important at that time?
subcategories.responding: What teaching did you provide, and how did you check understanding?
subcategories.reflecting: What would you do differently next time to improve patient learning?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you able to deliberately plan your teaching based on this patient's specific needs, or did you rely on standard handouts and general information? Are you beginning to organize your teaching around what this patient needs to know for discharge?

subcategories.title: Therapeutic Communication
subcategories.noticing: What communication cue, verbal or nonverbal, stood out to you?
subcategories.interpreting: What did that cue mean about what the patient needed from you?
subcategories.responding: What therapeutic communication strategy did you use?
subcategories.reflecting: How did the patient respond, and what did that reveal about your communication?
subcategories.selfCheck: Did you notice the patient responding to your communication in real time? Were you beginning to adapt your approach based on what you observed, or still relying on scripted techniques?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Apply basic leadership skills in the care of families.
questions: Noticing: What cues helped you recognize priorities? What did you anticipate when you first received report? How urgent did you expect the situation? What did you anticipate as highest priority?
Interpreting: How did you connect understanding of patient acuity with planning your approach? What are your priorities — most urgent concerns or actions? Who else might need to be involved?

Responding: How did you plan and implement leadership actions — organizing care, delegating tasks, managing time? What specific actions did you take? How do you provide comfort, safety, and symptom management?
Reflecting: Is the situation going as expected — what would you prefer to see happen? How did your priorities change? What did you learn from this situation or from collaborating with peers?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety, well-being, or overall care experience? 2. What patient responses confirmed, challenged, or refined your thinking? 3. Were there cues you initially overlooked or misinterpreted? 4. What would you do the same and what differently? 5. What does this experience show you about your strengths and your next step toward practice readiness?

subcategories.title: Demonstrates beginning leadership skills
subcategories.noticing: What in the situation required you to think about priorities, time, or coordination?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you decide what needed to happen first?
subcategories.responding: How did you show beginning leadership through organization, calm communication, or follow-through?
subcategories.reflecting: What did this situation teach you about leadership as a student nurse?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you engaged in organizing your day around patient needs and taking pride in that organization? Were you beginning to anticipate what needed to happen rather than only reacting? Did you find yourself checking repeatedly to make sure everything was done?

subcategories.title: Verbalizes appropriate tasks for delegation
subcategories.noticing: What task or need could reasonably be delegated in this situation?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was that task appropriate to delegate, and what should not be delegated?
subcategories.responding: How would you clearly explain the task, expected outcome, and timeframe?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about safe delegation from this experience?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to think about delegation deliberately, or was it still an abstract concept? Could you clearly explain why certain tasks can versus cannot be delegated?

subcategories.title: Verbalizes appropriate documentation of task delegation
subcategories.noticing: What part of the delegated task would need follow-up or accountability?
subcategories.interpreting: Why is documentation important when tasks are delegated?
subcategories.responding: What should be documented about the task, the communication, and the outcome?
subcategories.reflecting: How does documentation protect both the patient and the nurse?
subcategories.selfCheck: Are you beginning to organize your documentation deliberately, or is it still something you do after the fact? Can you connect documentation to patient safety, not just paperwork?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Practice as a member of a multi-disciplinary health care team.
questions: Noticing: What cues from team members, the patient, and the family helped you identify opportunities for collaboration? What is the patient/family particularly concerned about? What do you know that will impact care?

Interpreting: How did you integrate information from multiple team members to understand the full clinical picture? What do you think would be most important to the patient right now? What other perspectives did you consider?
Responding: Provide an SBAR that you will communicate to your health care team. What specific actions did you take? What patient preferences did you consider when prioritizing interventions?

Reflecting: How did the patient respond to interventions? How is your care aligning with the patient's preferences? What did you learn from collaborating with peers? What evidence informs you that your decisions worked out?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety, well-being, or overall care experience? 2. What patient responses confirmed, challenged, or refined your thinking? 3. Were there cues you initially overlooked or misinterpreted? 4. What would you do the same and what differently? 5. What does this experience show you about your strengths and your next step toward practice readiness?

subcategories.title: Interdisciplinary Communication
subcategories.noticing: What cues from the patient, family, or team told you communication was especially important here?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was structured communication (like SBAR) critical in this situation?
subcategories.responding: Provide an SBAR that you communicated (or would communicate) about your patient. How did team communication affect the care plan?
subcategories.reflecting: What feedback from the interaction shows your team communication was or was not effective?
subcategories.selfCheck: When you communicated urgency or concern, was your description specific enough that others understood and responded appropriately? At this stage, your ability to convey the clinical picture clearly is still developing — that's expected.

subcategories.title: Referral Sources
subcategories.noticing: What patient needs suggested a referral or additional team member was needed?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was that referral or resource important for this patient's outcome?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to connect the patient with the right resource or team member?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn about identifying and using referral sources in clinical practice?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to see beyond the immediate clinical needs to the patient's broader situation — discharge, follow-up, support — or was that still hard to see from your current vantage point?

subcategories.title: Clinical Judgment and Critical Thinking
subcategories.noticing: What clinical data or team input required you to think critically?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you weigh different perspectives or information to form your judgment?
subcategories.responding: How did you apply clinical judgment and feedback from team members to your care decisions?
subcategories.reflecting: What evidence do you recognize that informs you that your decisions worked out for the patient?
subcategories.selfCheck: Did forming your clinical judgment require conscious, deliberate effort — thinking it through step by step? Were you beginning to justify your reasoning, or did you rely mainly on what others told you to do?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Recognize benefits and limitations of community and governmental support for families.
questions: Noticing: What patient or family needs did you begin to organize into a broader understanding of their social situation? What specific issues must you account for? What else do you need to know? Talk about environmental factors that may interfere.

Interpreting: How did you connect knowledge of community resources with this patient and family's circumstances? Talk about cultural or social factors. What do you anticipate the patient will need beyond the immediate clinical environment?

Responding: How did you plan and implement resource referrals? What typical interventions for patients like this, and what changes specific to this patient? How do you prepare this patient and family for discharge?

Reflecting: How did your clinical decisions impact safety, well-being, or overall care? What were the outcomes? What gaps in your knowledge of community resources became apparent? What would you do differently?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety, well-being, or overall care experience? 2. What patient responses confirmed, challenged, or refined your thinking? 3. Were there cues you initially overlooked or misinterpreted? 4. What would you do the same and what differently? 5. What does this experience show you about your strengths and your next step toward practice readiness?

subcategories.title: Cultural factors
subcategories.noticing: What cultural or family factors affected this patient's understanding, decisions, or care?
subcategories.interpreting: How did those factors shape what the patient needed from the healthcare team?
subcategories.responding: How did you adapt communication, education, or care planning?
subcategories.reflecting: What did this teach you about culturally responsive continuity of care?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to know this patient's and family's priorities and what matters to them? Were you seeing culture as something that shapes the patient's experience, or still as a category on a form?

subcategories.title: Economic status
subcategories.noticing: What clues suggested finances, insurance, work, housing, transportation, or medication cost might be barriers?
subcategories.interpreting: How could those issues affect the patient's discharge plan or follow-up care?
subcategories.responding: What support, referral, or teaching would help reduce those barriers?
subcategories.reflecting: What did this situation teach you about the connection between economics and health outcomes?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to see the whole patient situation — life beyond the hospital — or was discharge planning still abstract? Could you anticipate barriers, or did they become apparent only when someone else pointed them out?

subcategories.title: Literacy
subcategories.noticing: What signs suggested a possible literacy, language, or health literacy issue?
subcategories.interpreting: How might limited understanding affect safety, adherence, or follow-up?
subcategories.responding: How did you adjust teaching or communication to match the patient's level of understanding?
subcategories.reflecting: What would you do differently next time to better assess understanding?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to adapt your communication to this specific patient, or did you rely on standard approaches that might not fit everyone? Did you check for understanding, or assume the patient understood?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Deliver family-centered care tailored to patient and family needs.
questions: Noticing: What patient or family needs required you to adapt your usual approach? What is the patient/family particularly concerned about? What cultural, economic, or social factors caught your attention?

Interpreting: How did you connect understanding of family-centered care with this specific patient and family? How did cultural, economic, or environmental factors influence priorities, coping, or decisions?
Responding: How did you adapt care to honor preferences and values? How did you move outside your comfort zone? What specific actions reflected collaboration with the client/family in care planning?
Reflecting: How did the patient and family respond? What did you learn about individualizing care beyond the diagnosis? Where did you recognize your own biases?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety, well-being, or overall care experience? 2. What patient responses confirmed, challenged, or refined your thinking? 3. Were there cues you initially overlooked or misinterpreted? 4. What would you do the same and what differently? 5. What does this experience show you about your strengths and your next step toward practice readiness?

subcategories.title: Plans, implements, and evaluates a plan of care
subcategories.noticing: What patient needs or problems were most important to organize first?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you connect patient data, diagnosis, and priorities into a care plan?
subcategories.responding: What interventions did you carry out, and what outcomes were you hoping to achieve?
subcategories.reflecting: How did the patient respond, and what would you revise in your plan of care next time?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to organize your care around plans and goals for this patient, or still working through tasks one at a time? Did you worry about missing something important — and did that help you be more thorough in your planning?

subcategories.title: Communicates effectively
subcategories.noticing: What communication needs did the patient have in this situation?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was communication especially important to safe or effective care here?
subcategories.responding: How did you communicate clearly with the patient, family, or team?
subcategories.reflecting: What feedback from the interaction shows your communication was or was not effective?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to notice that the patient responded to your communication — positively or negatively? Could you adjust in the moment, or did you realize what to change only afterward?

subcategories.title: Evaluates need for client education and provides teaching
subcategories.noticing: What made you realize the patient needed teaching?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you decide what the patient most needed to know at that moment?
subcategories.responding: What teaching did you provide, and how did you individualize it?
subcategories.reflecting: How do you know whether the teaching was effective?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you able to deliberately plan your teaching for this specific patient, or did you rely mostly on standard information? Are you beginning to connect patient education to the bigger picture of discharge readiness?

subcategories.title: Demonstrates respect, caring, and privacy
subcategories.noticing: What patient cue or situation reminded you that dignity and privacy were especially important?
subcategories.interpreting: Why were respect and caring central to this encounter?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to preserve privacy, comfort, and dignity?
subcategories.reflecting: How might the patient describe how you treated them?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to see this patient's pain, suffering, and vulnerability as something that matters to you personally — not just clinically? Are you moving beyond task completion toward genuine presence with the patient?

subcategories.title: Advocates for client needs
subcategories.noticing: What concern, risk, or unmet need required you to speak up?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was advocacy necessary in this situation?
subcategories.responding: What did you say or do to advocate for the patient?
subcategories.reflecting: What did this teach you about when and how to advocate more effectively?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you able to communicate the urgency of the patient's need clearly enough that others responded? Were you beginning to act on what the patient needed rather than waiting to be told?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Demonstrate therapeutic communication skills with families.
questions: Noticing: What communication cues — verbal and nonverbal — from the patient and family did you begin to organize? What is the patient/family particularly concerned about? What struck you about how they communicate?

Interpreting: How did you connect knowledge of therapeutic communication with the specific cultural and developmental context? Talk about cultural or social factors that may interfere with effective communication.

Responding: How did you plan and implement therapeutic communication deliberately — teach-back, open-ended questions, SBAR? How do you provide comfort through your communication specific to this patient?
Reflecting: How did the patient and family respond? What did their responses make you realize about your approach? What about your own communication skills and cultural biases became clearer?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety, well-being, or overall care experience? 2. What patient responses confirmed, challenged, or refined your thinking? 3. Were there cues you initially overlooked or misinterpreted? 4. What would you do the same and what differently? 5. What does this experience show you about your strengths and your next step toward practice readiness?

subcategories.title: Establishes, maintains, and terminates therapeutic relationships
subcategories.noticing: What did you notice about how the patient or family responded to you at the start of the interaction?
subcategories.interpreting: What did that tell you about how to build trust and maintain professional boundaries?
subcategories.responding: How did you begin, continue, and close the interaction therapeutically?
subcategories.reflecting: What would you improve next time about how you establish or end communication?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to notice that the patient responded to your interactions — and could you use that feedback to adjust your approach? Or were you still focused on what to say rather than how the patient was receiving it?

subcategories.title: Practices motivational interviewing
subcategories.noticing: What cues suggested the patient was uncertain, ambivalent, resistant, or not ready for change?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you make sense of the patient's readiness or hesitancy?
subcategories.responding: What open-ended questions, reflections, affirmations, or summaries did you use?
subcategories.reflecting: How did the patient respond to that approach, and what did you learn about guiding change talk?
subcategories.selfCheck: Did using MI techniques require conscious, deliberate effort — thinking about each question before you asked it? Were you beginning to adapt your approach based on the patient's responses, or still practicing the technique itself?

subcategories.title: Considers psychosocial and ego integrity when communicating
subcategories.noticing: What emotional, developmental, coping, or family-related cues did you observe?
subcategories.interpreting: How did those psychosocial factors shape what the patient or family needed from you?
subcategories.responding: How did you adjust your tone, words, pace, or presence to support emotional well-being?
subcategories.reflecting: What did this interaction teach you about communicating beyond the physical problem?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to see the person behind the clinical data — their fears, hopes, coping, and family? Or were psychosocial needs still secondary to physical care in your awareness?

subcategories.title: Demonstrates cultural awareness when communicating
subcategories.noticing: What cultural, language, or value-based communication factors were present?
subcategories.interpreting: How did those factors influence how the patient or family understood you?
subcategories.responding: What did you do to make your communication more respectful, clear, and culturally aware?
subcategories.reflecting: What assumptions or habits in your communication style became visible through this interaction?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to know what matters to this patient and family — their values, preferences, and priorities? Or were cultural considerations still something you thought about in general rather than for this specific person?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Implement evidence-based, safe care using clinical judgment and technology.
questions: Noticing: What patterns in patient data did you begin to organize into meaningful observations? What cues or signs caught your attention — what do they mean? What were you expecting to find? What stood out as most important?
Interpreting: How did you begin to connect data and patient cues into meaningful clinical patterns? What key hunches did you have, and what confirmed or disconfirmed them? How did you prioritize?

Responding: What specific clinical actions did you take, and how did they align with patient needs? What outcomes were you hoping for? Identify and discuss nursing interventions needed to prevent 'never events.'
Reflecting: What were the outcomes compared to expectations? Were there cues you initially overlooked? What did you learn that might help next time? How did your decisions impact safety and care?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety, well-being, or overall care experience? 2. What patient responses confirmed, challenged, or refined your thinking? 3. Were there cues you initially overlooked or misinterpreted? 4. What would you do the same and what differently? 5. What does this experience show you about your strengths and your next step toward practice readiness?

subcategories.title: Anticipates, observes, and monitors subjective and objective data
subcategories.noticing: What subjective and objective data were you paying close attention to in this patient?
subcategories.interpreting: What did those findings make you think might be happening?
subcategories.responding: How did you continue to monitor the patient, and what were you specifically watching for?
subcategories.reflecting: Looking back, was there any important data you should have assessed earlier or more often?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to organize your observations into a whole clinical picture, or were you still assessing data points individually? Could you anticipate the expected findings for this patient, or were you unsure what to expect?

subcategories.title: Recognizes patterns and deviations in data
subcategories.noticing: What findings seemed "off," out of the ordinary, or different from what you expected?
subcategories.interpreting: What pattern did you begin to see when you looked at the data together instead of separately?
subcategories.responding: How did those patterns influence your ongoing assessment or reassessment?
subcategories.reflecting: Were there subtle cues you initially missed or underestimated, and how would recognizing them sooner have changed your thinking?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to grasp this situation as a whole — seeing the big picture rather than just parts? Was this a familiar clinical picture where you could anticipate concerns, or unfamiliar territory where you were working to make sense of it?

subcategories.title: Seeks subjective information from patient and family
subcategories.noticing: What did the patient or family tell you that helped you better understand the situation?
subcategories.interpreting: Why was that subjective information important when compared with the objective findings?
subcategories.responding: How did you use the patient's or family's input when deciding what care or intervention was needed?
subcategories.reflecting: What else should you have asked the patient or family to better guide your care plan?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to know this patient's obvious patterns and what matters to them, or were you still focused mainly on the chart data? Did the patient's own words change how you understood the clinical picture?

subcategories.title: Focuses on the most important data and seeks further information
subcategories.noticing: Which findings were the most salient or clinically important in this situation?
subcategories.interpreting: How did you decide what mattered most and what could wait?
subcategories.responding: What additional assessment, question, or data did you seek to better understand the situation?
subcategories.reflecting: Did you spend too much attention on less important details, or did you stay focused on the priority problem?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to see which data was most clinically relevant and which could wait, or did everything still feel equally important? Are you developing a sense of salience — knowing what stands out?

subcategories.title: Interprets data patterns to develop an intervention plan
subcategories.noticing: What patient findings began to fit a recognizable clinical picture for you?
subcategories.interpreting: What hunches or hypotheses did you have, and what findings confirmed or disconfirmed them?
subcategories.responding: Based on that pattern, what intervention plan made the most sense?
subcategories.reflecting: How accurate was your interpretation, and what did this teach you about linking cues to likely problems?
subcategories.selfCheck: With conscious, deliberate effort, were you able to connect your theoretical knowledge to what you observed in this patient? Were you beginning to justify your reasoning — even to yourself — rather than just following instructions?

subcategories.title: Develops interventions and monitors progress
subcategories.noticing: What patient data most directly guided your intervention choices?
subcategories.interpreting: Why were those interventions appropriate for this particular patient at that time?
subcategories.responding: What actions did you take, what outcomes were you hoping for, and how did you monitor whether the patient was improving or worsening?
subcategories.reflecting: How did the patient respond, and what did that response make you realize about your reasoning?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were your interventions planned deliberately based on what you knew about this patient, or were you primarily reacting to what happened? Are you beginning to organize your care around plans and anticipated outcomes rather than just completing tasks?

subcategories.title: Performs nursing skills safely while promoting caring behaviors
subcategories.noticing: What safety risks, precautions, or possible complications did you need to keep in mind while providing care?
subcategories.interpreting: What made those safety concerns especially important for this patient?
subcategories.responding: How did you perform your skills in a way that was safe, efficient, and still caring? What did you do to prevent "never events" or avoidable complications?
subcategories.reflecting: Did your care support both safety and comfort, or was there anything you would change in how you carried out the skill?
subcategories.selfCheck: Were you beginning to anticipate complications before they happened and plan to prevent them? Or did safety concerns primarily come to your attention after something went wrong or was pointed out? Were you able to be both safe AND caring, or did focusing on safety crowd out caring presence?

subcategories.title: Evaluates personal clinical performance
subcategories.noticing: What major clinical event, decision, or turning point in the situation stands out most to you?
subcategories.interpreting: Why did you make the decisions you made at that moment?
subcategories.responding: What did you do that was helpful, and what might you have done differently?
subcategories.reflecting: What did you learn from this situation that will guide your future practice, clinical judgment, and patient safety decisions?
subcategories.selfCheck: Are you beginning to evaluate your own clinical reasoning — connecting what you thought, what you did, and how the patient responded? Do you feel you had enough knowledge for this situation, or are there gaps that concern you? That awareness of your own gaps is itself a sign of growth.

reflectionTemplates.focus: Analyze ethical and legal concerns experienced by clients, families, and nurses associated with acute and chronic client care, including the dying process.
questions: A. Demonstrates ethical behavior in accord with ANA Code of Ethics — Noticing: What ethical, legal, or regulatory concern stood out most clearly, and whose interests were affected? Interpreting: How did you make sense of the dilemma, including conflict between patient rights, protocols, safety, or the greater good? Responding: How did you act, communicate, or advocate reflecting the ANA Code of Ethics? Reflecting: What did this teach you about recognizing and articulating ethical dilemmas?

B. Applies ethical principles to identify choices and consequences — Noticing: What decision point required you to consider ethical choices and consequences? Interpreting: Which ethical principles were most relevant, and how did they shape reasoning? Responding: What option did you support, and how did you think through consequences? Reflecting: How well did your reasoning account for immediate and longer-term consequences?

C. Engages in reflection about choices, frameworks, and implications — Noticing: What choice or action feels most important to reflect on now? Interpreting: How do you understand that decision through an ethical framework? Responding: What would you keep the same or change? Reflecting: What did this teach you about how your ethical reasoning is developing toward the RN role?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety, well-being, or overall care experience? 2. What patient responses or outcomes confirmed, challenged, or refined your thinking? 3. In hindsight, were there any cues you initially overlooked or misinterpreted? 4. If faced with a similar situation again, what would you do the same and what would you do differently? 5. What does this experience show you about your strengths and your next step toward practice readiness?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Propose adjustments to the plan of care after reflecting on clients level of comfort and ability to manage symptoms.
questions: A. Examines personal and professional patterns; practices self-reflection — Noticing: What about patient comfort, distress, or symptom pattern made you reconsider the plan? Interpreting: How did you connect what you observed with your own clinical habits, assumptions, or patterns? Responding: What adjustment did you make or propose? Reflecting: What did this teach you about how self-reflection can improve outcomes?

B. Seeks and responds to constructive feedback — Noticing: What made feedback especially important? Interpreting: How did the feedback help you better understand the patient's needs or your actions? Responding: How did you use that feedback to modify care, and how did you provide feedback to others? Reflecting: What did this teach you about receiving and using feedback professionally?

C. Acknowledges implications for personal and professional growth — Noticing: What did this situation reveal about your readiness, confidence, or limitation? Interpreting: Why is this insight important for both personal and professional growth? Responding: What will you intentionally work on next? Reflecting: How is this experience shaping the kind of nurse you are becoming?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Incorporate evidence-based interventions in providing care to groups of clients in acute care and mental health settings.
questions: A. Takes responsibility for learning needs; selects challenging experiences — Noticing: What showed you that deeper learning was needed? Interpreting: Why did this push you beyond routine care? Responding: How did you take initiative to seek learning or engage in complex care? Reflecting: What did this teach you about owning your learning at the N360 level?

B. Evaluates evidence supporting opinions and practice — Noticing: What intervention or usual practice made you stop and ask what evidence supported it? Interpreting: How did you weigh available evidence, patient context, and clinical reasoning? Responding: What evidence-based intervention did you support or perform, and why? Reflecting: What did this teach you about not accepting practice at face value?

C. Participates in additional learning; seeks information beyond assignments — Noticing: What question or uncertainty was not fully answered by routine preparation? Interpreting: Why was it important to investigate further? Responding: What additional learning did you pursue, and how did it affect your care? Reflecting: What did this teach you about self-directed learning as part of safe practice?

D. Volunteers to assist peers and contributes to group learning — Noticing: What learning need did you notice in your peers or clinical group? Interpreting: Why did supporting group learning matter? Responding: How did you assist peers or strengthen team readiness? Reflecting: What did this teach you about leadership through shared learning?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Use management principles, strategies, and tools in caring for a group of patients.
questions: A. Identifies effective leadership; understands consequences with limited information — Noticing: What required leadership rather than just task completion? Interpreting: How did limited information affect your judgment or need to gather more data? Responding: What leadership behaviors did you demonstrate to keep care organized, safe, and moving forward? Reflecting: What did this teach you about making leadership decisions under uncertainty?

B. Prioritizes actions, organizes care, ensures comprehensive care, meets time commitments — Noticing: What priorities competed for your attention, and what made some more urgent? Interpreting: How did you decide what needed to happen first, what could wait, what needed follow-up? Responding: How did you organize your time, assessments, and follow-through? Reflecting: What did this teach you about managing multiple patients more like an RN?

C. Delegates ensuring scope, competency, clear communication, and agreement — Noticing: What task could appropriately be delegated? Interpreting: How did you determine whether delegation was safe, appropriate, and within scope? Responding: How did you delegate clearly, confirm understanding, and follow up? Reflecting: What did this teach you about delegation as a clinical judgment responsibility?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Work collaboratively with the healthcare team to create individualized care plans that promote health and well-being.
questions: A. Actively contributes to teamwork; offers help, shares information, consults — Noticing: What cues showed this situation required active teamwork and consultation? Interpreting: How did team input change, strengthen, or clarify your understanding? Responding: How did you contribute to problem-solving, information sharing, or decision-making? Reflecting: What did this teach you about your role in coordinated care?

B. Maintains positive relationships; works with varying points of view — Noticing: What differences in perspective, style, or priority emerged? Interpreting: How did those differences affect care planning or team function? Responding: How did you maintain a positive working relationship while contributing meaningfully? Reflecting: What did this teach you about working with different viewpoints?

C. Asks for assistance and feedback; gives feedback appropriately — Noticing: What made assistance or feedback necessary? Interpreting: Why was it important to involve others rather than work in isolation? Responding: How did you seek help, receive feedback, or provide feedback? Reflecting: What did this teach you about humility, accountability, and team communication?

D. Recognizes need to manage health barriers and stressors; sets priorities — Noticing: What personal or team stressors affected the situation? Interpreting: How did those stressors influence your priorities or ability to function? Responding: What did you do to stay focused and continue providing safe care? Reflecting: What did this teach you about managing yourself as part of safe team practice?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Assist clients to obtain community health resources for health promotion and maintenance.
questions: A. Recognizes current and needed resources — Noticing: What unmet need made you recognize the patient required resources beyond bedside care? Interpreting: How did you determine which resources were available and most relevant? Responding: What resource, referral, or support did you help identify? Reflecting: What did this teach you about seeing resource needs as part of nursing care?

B. Makes contacts with community agencies; assists clients to reduce barriers — Noticing: What barrier to access, follow-up, or treatment stood out? Interpreting: How did that barrier affect continuity of care? Responding: With faculty permission, how did you assist with connecting the patient to services? Reflecting: What did this teach you about nursing action beyond the unit?

C. Identifies impact of laws, regulations on resource availability — Noticing: What system issue, rule, or structural barrier affected resource availability? Interpreting: How did you connect that system factor to the patient's actual options? Responding: How did you account for those realities when planning care or referrals? Reflecting: What did this teach you about how systems shape patient outcomes?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Demonstrate safe and effective clinical judgment and use of technology in the management of client care.
questions: A. Notes and makes sense of patterns in client data — Noticing: What cues, trends, or data patterns stood out as most clinically significant? Interpreting: How did you compare what you were seeing with known patterns from nursing knowledge, research, experience, or intuition? Responding: How did that interpretation shape your assessment priorities or next action? Reflecting: What did this teach you about moving from isolated findings to meaningful clinical patterns?

B. Develops intervention plans justified by likelihood for success — Noticing: What patient needs or risks most clearly drove your intervention planning? Interpreting: How did you determine which intervention was most likely to succeed for this patient? Responding: What plan did you develop, and what evidence or reasoning supported it? Reflecting: How well did your plan fit the patient's condition and likely trajectory?

C. Explains interventions; calms/reassures; directs team; checks understanding — Noticing: What did the patient, family, or team need to understand for care to proceed safely? Interpreting: Why was explanation, reassurance, direction, or checking understanding especially important? Responding: How did you explain, calm, direct, and verify understanding? Reflecting: What did this teach you about communication as part of clinical judgment?

D. Tailors interventions; monitors progress; adjusts treatment — Noticing: What patient-specific factor made individualized treatment especially important? Interpreting: How did you decide the patient's response required change or continuation? Responding: How did you tailor the intervention and monitor closely? Reflecting: What did this teach you about adapting care based on real patient response?

E. Independently evaluates outcomes and client response; revises plan — Noticing: What outcome told you whether the plan was working? Interpreting: How did you make sense of that outcome in relation to your original reasoning? Responding: How did you evaluate outcomes independently and with the team, and revise the plan? Reflecting: What did this teach you about competent-to-practice-ready clinical judgment?

CLOSING PROMPTS: 1. How did your clinical decisions affect the patient's safety, well-being, or overall care experience? 2. What patient responses or outcomes confirmed, challenged, or refined your thinking? 3. In hindsight, were there any cues you initially overlooked or misinterpreted? 4. If faced with a similar situation again, what would you do the same and what would you do differently? 5. What does this experience show you about your strengths and your next step toward practice readiness?

reflectionTemplates.focus: Utilize a systematic approach to develop and deliver a plan of care.

questions.title: Anticipates, observes, and monitors data; recognizes patterns and deviations
questions.noticing: What patient data were you paying close attention to?
questions.interpreting: What made those findings seem important?
questions.responding: What did you continue to observe, recheck, or report?
questions.reflecting: What did this teach you about careful observation?

questions.title: Seeks subjective information from patient and family; focuses on priority data
questions.noticing: What did the patient or family tell you that seemed important?
questions.interpreting: Why did that information matter along with the assessment data?
questions.responding: How did you use what they told you when planning or reporting care?
questions.reflecting: What did this teach you about listening to the patient and family?

questions.title: Interprets data patterns; develops intervention plan with rationale
questions.noticing: What patient finding reminded you of something you learned in class, lab, or prior clinical?
questions.interpreting: How did that prior knowledge help you make basic sense of the situation?
questions.responding: What standard action did you take, or whose guidance did you seek?
questions.reflecting: What did this teach you about connecting classroom learning to patient care?

questions.title: Demonstrates effective nursing skills with safety; evaluates own clinical performance
questions.noticing: What major event or decision stands out most to you now?
questions.interpreting: Why do you think that moment mattered?
questions.responding: What did you do, and what other option did you consider?
questions.reflecting: What would you do the same or differently next time?

closingPrompts: What did I notice first in this situation?
What guidance, rule, policy, or resource helped me most?
What did I do, and how did the patient respond?
What did I learn from this experience?
What would I do differently next time?
What do I still need to learn more about?

logbookEntries.note: First clinical day — on time, prepared.

logbookEntries.title: First LTC shift — patient with mid-stage dementia

logbookEntries.note: Competent — task completion solid; building confidence with families.
De-escalated agitated patient family member with empathy and clarity.
Called in sick — documented per program policy, not pattern.
Caught own error before administering wrong dose. Self-reported. Good clinical judgment under pressure — counted as STRENGTH not failure.
Locked end-of-semester; consistent reflective practice + strong professionalism + perfect attendance pattern.

logbookEntries.title: Ethical dilemma — patient refusing pain medication

logbookEntries.note: Proficient — strong ethical reasoning; pushed back appropriately on team decision.

logbookEntries.title: Care plan for post-op patient with complications

logbookEntries.note: Beginner — hesitant to delegate; works through it with debrief.
Took initiative to brief next shift on patient priorities — beyond what assignment required.
Competent — delegated wound care to LPN appropriately, followed up.
Late charting on med pass — patient unaffected, addressed in shift huddle. Pattern check at end of term.
Strong growth across leadership + judgment. Professionalism trending up. Locked end-of-semester.

logbookEntries.title: Triage decision-making in fast-paced ER environment
Patient family meeting — managing emotional load (overdue grading)

logbookEntries.note: Beginner — hesitant to delegate medication pass during full assignment; defaulted to doing everything himself.
Beginner — froze when sim instructor introduced second admission; needed prompt to call for help.
Competent — delegated vitals to LPN; followed up appropriately.
